title: Sticky ginger cake
subtitle: Dark treacle and golden syrup ginger traybake
excerpt: A dark, moist ginger cake made with black treacle and golden syrup, baked in a rectangular tin and cut into squares. The treacle keeps the crumb sticky for five days. Generously spiced with ground ginger and mixed spice. Better on day two than day one.
sourceNotes: Dark ginger cake made with treacle is documented in British domestic cooking from the mid-nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the gingerbread and ginger cake chapter documents a treacle-based ginger cake baked in a tin and cut into squares, noting that the treacle keeps the cake moist over several days. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the sweet bread chapter includes a dark ginger cake with black treacle and ground ginger, baked slowly at a low heat to prevent the treacle from burning.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into squares and freeze in a bag. Defrost at room temperature for 1 hour — the stickiness returns as it warms.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps wrapped at room temperature for 5 days, becoming stickier and more intensely flavoured over time. Do not refrigerate — the cold dries the crumb.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Low temperature is essential — treacle burns at high heat and will produce a bitter crust

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake batter made with black treacle and golden syrup in place of most of the sugar. The sugars in treacle and syrup are hygroscopic — they absorb moisture from the air — which is why a treacle cake stays sticky and moist for days rather than drying out. The bicarbonate of soda reacts with the acidity in the treacle to produce lift, replacing the role of baking powder.

content.text: The characteristic of a good sticky ginger cake is the second day: on day one it is dense and warm-spiced; by day two the treacle has made the crumb stickier and the ginger more pronounced. The method is the same as all melted-butter cakes: melt the fats and sugars together, beat in the eggs, fold in the dry ingredients. The batter is liquid and dark and smells powerfully of treacle.

Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Butter and line the rectangular tin. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{black-treacle}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{dark-brown-sugar}} together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Do not let the mixture boil. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{whole-milk}}, then the beaten {{eggs}}. This is the
: it will be dark, liquid, and strongly scented.

In a large bowl, combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Pour the warm treacle mixture into the dry ingredients and stir until smooth. The batter will be thin.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 38–42 minutes until the top feels firm when pressed lightly and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The cake will sink slightly in the centre as it cools — this is normal. Cool completely in the tin before cutting. The cake will be stickier and more flavourful the following day.
